name: Amabie
shortDescription: A mermaid-like yokai who prophesies plagues and instructs people to copy and share its image as protection.
description: Amabie is a Japanese yokai (supernatural creature) that emerged into public awareness in the Edo period. It is depicted as a mermaid-like creature with long hair, scales, and three legs or fins. Amabie is known for its prophecy of impending epidemics or plagues. According to the legend, it appeared to a government official in Higo Province (present-day Kumamoto Prefecture) in 1846 and delivered a prophecy stating that a plague would soon spread throughout the land. To ward off the disease, Amabie instructed people to copy its image and share it with others. This act of sharing was believed to have protective power against the impending illness. The Amabie's story was disseminated through kawaraban (woodblock-printed news sheets), which were an early form of mass media in Japan. In modern times, Amabie has experienced a resurgence in popularity as a symbol of protection against the COVID-19 pandemic, with artists and others creating and sharing Amabie images online.
subtitle: Prophet of plagues, protector through art.
appearance: Amabie is depicted with long hair, a mouth resembling a bird's beak, and a body covered in scales. It has three legs or fins. Its overall appearance is often described as mermaid-like, though with unique characteristics.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Amabie legend originates from a single recorded instance in 1846, reported on a kawaraban. According to the account, a luminous object appeared in the sea off Higo Province. When investigated, it revealed itself to be an Amabie, who delivered a prophecy about an upcoming period of disease. The Amabie instructed that its image be drawn and displayed to ward off the illness. This event, and the subsequent dissemination of Amabie's image, is the primary source for the yokai's mythology. Unlike many yokai with deep roots in folklore, Amabie's origin is relatively recent and well-documented, tied directly to the kawaraban report. This single appearance solidified its role as a prophetic entity linked to disease and protection.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Amabie's role is primarily that of a prophetic messenger and a protector against disease. Its significance lies in its ability to provide hope and a tangible means of coping with epidemics. By instructing people to copy its image, Amabie offered a way to actively participate in their own protection. In times of uncertainty and fear, the Amabie image served as a visual talisman, fostering a sense of community and shared purpose. The yokai's significance has been amplified in modern times, with its image being widely circulated during the COVID-19 pandemic as a symbol of resilience and a reminder of the power of collective action against adversity.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Amabie's symbolism is multifaceted. It represents both the threat of disease and the potential for overcoming it. The act of copying and sharing its image symbolizes the importance of community solidarity and the power of collective action in the face of adversity. The Amabie can be interpreted as a reminder that even in the darkest of times, hope and protection can be found in unexpected places. Its unique appearance, combining human and animal features, may also symbolize the interconnectedness of all living things and the need for harmony between humans and the natural world. The act of creating and sharing the image can be seen as a form of folk magic, where the belief in the image's power helps to manifest the desired outcome of protection.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Amabie's legacy is primarily tied to its resurgence in popularity during the COVID-19 pandemic. The yokai's image has been widely shared on social media, inspiring countless artists, designers, and craftspeople to create their own interpretations of the creature. This has led to the production of Amabie-themed merchandise, artwork, and even public health campaigns. The Amabie has become a symbol of hope, resilience, and community spirit during a time of global crisis. Its story has also helped to raise awareness of Japanese folklore and the rich tradition of yokai. The Amabie's modern revival demonstrates the enduring power of folklore to provide comfort, inspiration, and a sense of connection in the face of uncertainty.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: Original news sheet reporting the Amabie's appearance and prophecy.

sources.author: Shigeru Mizuki
sources.description: Mizuki's illustrated encyclopedia of yokai includes a depiction and description of Amabie.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Its aquatic nature and appearance from the sea.

associations.name: Disease
associations.significance: Directly associated with prophesying and warding off disease.

associations.name: Protection
associations.significance: Its image is believed to offer protection against illness.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Sirens
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are aquatic creatures with human-like features and the power to influence humans.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Nereids
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Sea nymphs who could be benevolent or dangerous, similar to Amabie's dual association with disease and protection.

companions.name: Amabiko
companions.description: Another prophetic yokai from the same period, also depicted as having multiple legs and associated with disease.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Amabie has seen a massive resurgence in popularity during the COVID-19 pandemic. Its image is widely circulated online as a symbol of protection and hope, with artists creating countless variations and interpretations. Amabie has become a cultural icon representing resilience and collective action against adversity.
